title: Best Coffee Scales 2026
description: Coffee scales with built-in timers compared — Acaia Pearl, Timemore Black Mirror Basic+, Hario V60 Drip Scale, Felicita Incline, and Coffee Gator pour-over scale — on response time, timer integration, durability, and what separates a purpose-built coffee scale from a kitchen scale.
lede: A coffee scale is not just a kitchen scale — the critical difference is response time and a built-in timer. The grind, water temp, and ratio matter far more than which brewer you choose.

sections.heading: Why response time matters for pour-over brewing
paragraphs: Response time and pour control: when pouring into a V60 or Chemex, water weight is increasing continuously. With a 2-second lag, you read 60g, the scale shows 60g 2 seconds later, and you've already poured 70g. A 0.1-0.5 second response time means what you see on the display reflects near-real-time weight — you can pour to a target weight by watching the number approach and stopping in time. This precision matters most for bloom weight (typically 2× coffee weight, ±2g) and subsequent pour targets in recipes that specify exact intervals.

Timer integration: most coffee scale recipes specify total brew time and pour intervals — for example: bloom at 0:00, first pour at 0:45, second pour at 1:30, target finish by 3:30. Running a phone stopwatch while simultaneously watching a scale display and controlling a pour is awkward. An integrated timer starts with a single button press and displays both weight and time on the same screen. Some scales (Acaia, Felicita) have auto-start timers that begin when weight is detected, removing the need to manually start the timer with wet hands.

0.1g resolution: most coffee scales display to 0.1g resolution. Kitchen scales typically display to 1g or at best 0.5g. The 0.1g resolution matters most for espresso — 0.2g difference in espresso dose can affect extraction visibly. For pour-over, 0.5g precision is usually sufficient for practical brewing. Scales that read to 0.1g but have slow response time are less useful than scales with faster response at 0.5g resolution — response time is the more important spec for pour-over.

sections.heading: Acaia Pearl and Felicita: the premium options
paragraphs: Acaia Pearl ($180-220) is the reference coffee scale — the standard against which other coffee scales are measured. It combines 0.1g resolution, 300ms response time, and an integrated timer with Bluetooth connectivity to the Acaia app (which logs brews and provides guided recipes). The Pearl is available in two sizes: Pearl (18cm × 18cm platform, 2kg max) and Pearl S (14cm × 14cm, for espresso machines with limited drip tray space). Build quality is aluminum with no moving parts except the power/timer buttons. Water resistance is adequate for coffee splashes but not submersion. The Pearl justifies its premium price through longevity — well-maintained Acaias last 5-10 years.

Felicita Incline ($80-120) is the most distinctive design in the category — the display is tilted at a 10-degree angle toward the user, making it readable over a portafilter or dripper without having to lean forward. The Incline uses a rechargeable battery (USB-C), has auto-start timer, and 0.1g resolution at approximately 200-300ms response time. The Incline is positioned as a premium feature alternative to the Acaia at significantly lower cost. Some users find the tilted display gimmicky; others find it genuinely useful for espresso setups where the scale is recessed under an espresso machine's drip tray.

Both Acaia and Felicita use rechargeable batteries, which is a meaningful advantage over AA-battery scales for daily use. Battery life on both is approximately 5-8 hours of continuous use per charge. The Acaia's Bluetooth app connectivity is useful for documenting brew parameters — if you're iterating on a recipe, logging dose, yield, and time across multiple attempts simplifies improvement.

sections.heading: Timemore Black Mirror and Hario V60: the mid-range options
paragraphs: Timemore Black Mirror Basic+ ($40-60) is the best-value dedicated coffee scale — it provides 0.1g resolution, a built-in timer, and approximately 500ms response time at a fraction of the Acaia price. The Basic+ display is easy to read, the timer starts with a single button, and the scale uses AA batteries (widely available, easy to replace while traveling). Build quality is plastic with a metal platform surface — it's lighter and less premium feeling than the Acaia but functionally adequate for pour-over brewing. For home brewers who want accurate coffee measurement without the Acaia premium, the Timemore Black Mirror is the standard recommendation.

Hario V60 Drip Scale ($50-70) is designed specifically for the Hario V60 ecosystem but works with any pour-over. The Hario scale emphasizes simplicity: one button starts the timer, it reads to 0.1g, and has a response time of approximately 1 second — slightly slower than Timemore but still faster than a kitchen scale. The display is clear with a simple white readout on black background. Battery life is approximately 80 hours on AA batteries — significantly longer than rechargeable scales. For brewers who already use V60 equipment and want a cohesive setup, the Hario scale is the natural complement, though the Timemore provides better response time at similar price.

Coffee Gator Pour-Over Scale ($25-40) is the budget option — it includes a timer, reads to 0.1g, and runs on AA batteries. Response time is approximately 1-2 seconds, which is adequate for casual pour-over brewing but less precise than the Timemore for recipe adherence. The Coffee Gator is a significant step up from a kitchen scale for coffee use at a modest price. For beginners who want to add measurement to their pour-over routine without spending $40+, the Coffee Gator provides the essential functionality. Experienced brewers will notice the slower response time when pouring to exact weights.

sections.heading: Scale selection for espresso vs pour-over
paragraphs: Espresso-specific requirements: espresso scales need to fit on an espresso machine's drip tray, which often has limited space — some machines have trays that are only 150mm × 150mm or smaller. The Acaia Pearl S, Felicita Incline, and Timemore Black Mirror Basic+ are all compact enough for most home espresso machines. Espresso also benefits more from 0.1g resolution than pour-over does — a 0.2g dose difference at 18g is 1.1%, which is detectable. For espresso, the scale sits underneath the portafilter during dosing and under the cup during extraction — it needs to be active for longer continuous use than pour-over.

Pour-over-specific requirements: for pour-over, scale size is less critical because you have more platform space. Response time is the key spec — the faster the scale, the more accurately you can hit pour targets in a recipe. For a V60 or Chemex recipe, a 0.5-1 second response time is workable; for strict recipe adherence, 300ms or faster is preferable. Platform weight capacity matters: most pour-over vessels + water can weigh 400-600g, which is within every scale's capacity. The max capacity spec only matters if you're using very large vessels.

Kitchen scale vs coffee scale: if you already own a kitchen scale and are starting with pour-over, try your kitchen scale first. If the lag makes it difficult to hit targets, upgrade to a dedicated coffee scale. The clearest signal that you need a dedicated scale: you're consistently overshooting your target weights because you can't stop pouring before the number catches up.

faqs.q: Do I need a coffee scale for pour-over?
faqs.a: Not strictly required — many experienced brewers develop a feel for water volume by eye or use measuring cups. But a scale is the most reliable way to achieve consistent, reproducible results. Recipe adherence (1:15 ratio, specific bloom weight, timed pour intervals) requires knowing exact weights as you brew. A coffee scale costs $25-220 and is the single highest-impact equipment upgrade for most pour-over brewers who aren't already using one — the improvement in consistency is immediate.

faqs.q: What's the difference between response time and accuracy?
faqs.a: Accuracy (resolution) refers to the smallest weight increment the scale can measure — 0.1g vs 1g. Response time refers to how quickly the displayed weight updates after a weight change occurs. A scale can be accurate (0.1g resolution) but slow (2 second lag), which makes it difficult to pour to precise weights in real time. Most dedicated coffee scales offer both 0.1g resolution and fast response time. For coffee brewing, response time is the more critical spec — a 0.5g-resolution scale with 200ms response is more useful than a 0.1g scale with 2 second lag.

faqs.q: Can I use a coffee scale for espresso if I already have a pour-over scale?
faqs.a: Usually yes, with one important check: make sure the scale fits on your espresso machine's drip tray. Measure the available tray space before assuming a scale will fit. The Acaia Pearl (18cm × 18cm) is too large for some home espresso machines; the Pearl S (14cm × 14cm) or Timemore Black Mirror are compact enough for most. Functionality-wise, the same scale works for both applications — the timer and weight measurement serve both purposes.

offerNotes.acaia-pearl-coffee-scale: 0.1g resolution, 300ms response, Bluetooth, rechargeable, two sizes. $180-220. Reference coffee scale — best build quality, Bluetooth app connectivity, longest longevity. Premium price justified by 5-10 year lifespan. Standard recommendation for serious brewers.
offerNotes.timemore-black-mirror-scale: 0.1g resolution, 500ms response, AA batteries, timer. $40-60. Best value dedicated coffee scale — provides all essential coffee scale features at budget-friendly price. AA batteries convenient for travel. The default recommendation for home pour-over.
offerNotes.hario-v60-drip-scale: 0.1g resolution, 1 second response, AA batteries, 80-hour battery life. $50-70. Best V60 companion scale — very long battery life, simple operation. Slightly slower response than Timemore. Correct for V60 ecosystem users who prioritize simplicity.
offerNotes.felicita-incline-coffee-scale: 0.1g resolution, 200-300ms response, USB-C rechargeable, tilted display, auto-start timer. $80-120. Best ergonomic design — tilted display readable over espresso portafilter. Useful for espresso setups with recessed drip trays.
offerNotes.coffee-gator-pour-over-scale: 0.1g resolution, 1-2 second response, AA batteries, timer. $25-40. Budget entry-level option — provides timer and measurement at lowest cost. Slower response limits precision. Correct for beginners adding measurement to pour-over routine.

pinDescription: Coffee scale 2026: Acaia Pearl vs Timemore Black Mirror vs Hario V60 vs Felicita Incline — why response time matters more than resolution, built-in timer explained, espresso vs pour-over scale requirements, and the best coffee scale at every price point.
methodology: We assessed each product on flavor profile, sourcing transparency, value per serving, packaging integrity, and how well it performed across common use cases. Documented certifications and verified user reviews were cross-checked against marketing claims.
